topic.title: Stylesheet Parameters on the Command Line

body.p:
ParameterizationStylesheet Parameters When calling XSLT on the entry file (main stylesheet), parameters can be passed on the command line. These are normally declared in the main stylesheet, but can also be placed in sub-stylesheetsParameterizationSub-Stylesheet
if they should only be locally available.
UWE test footnote (31/40, callout E): DITA fn maps to UWE footnote/key/desc.
A parameter can look like this, for example:
If the @required field is set to the value yes, then no default value may be specified in the @select attribute. (The default could of course also be placed as PCDATA content within the tags.)
A Saxon invocation on the command line that supplies this parameter looks like this, for example:

section.title: XPath as Parameter Value
section.p: There are also some hidden functionalities here, for example, you may want to pass an XPath expression to the stylesheet that the main program perhaps computes with the help of a database query.
In the simplest case, such an XPath is a boolean value true(). This is correctly passed to the stylesheet by prepending the XPath identifier ?, like this:
Because of the ? prefix, Saxon immediately understands that it is an XPath. An xsl:choose statement like the following would yield the correct branch:
If you did not set the prefix and sent, for example, this name=value pair:
The good branch would also be executed, because the non-empty string would be interpreted as true, which is incorrect.
You can of course also program in a type-safe manner and attach a type declaration to the parameter, which facilitates debugging:
To show a more complex scenario for a passed XPath, consider the following parameter passing:

The shell variable $id-aus-webservice-abfrage is populated during execution of the shell script with the result value of a preceding web service call and passed to the XSLT stylesheet. This operates on a book list and formats the selected book, for example, as an HTML page:

section.title: Clark Notation
section.p: Clark NotationWith Clark Notation James Clark (XML Namespaces), the required namespace can be prepended to the element name using curly braces. This looks, for example, as follows - if you also prepend the ? prefix:
In the called stylesheet, first the namespace is declared, then the parameter is set, and finally the logic is specified:
section.pre: <xsl:stylesheet xmlns:xsl="http://www.w3.org/1999/XSL/Transform" xmlns:xs="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema" xmlns:tk="http://www.tekturcms.de/namespaces/mein-namespace" exclude-result-prefixes="xs" version="2.0"> <xsl:param name="tk:spezieller-verarbeitungs-modus" select="false()" as="xs:boolean"/> <xsl:template name="main"> <xsl:choose> <xsl:when test="$tk:spezieller-verarbeitungs-modus">Gut</xsl:when> <xsl:otheriwse>Schlecht</xsl:otheriwse> </xsl:choose> </xsl:template> </xsl:stylesheet>
section.p: This special case is really only needed when you want to introduce a parameter into an existing transformation whose name already exists, in order to avoid a naming conflict - seen for example in complex Schematron rules.
